content.text: A Minimum Spanning Tree (MST) is a subset of edges in a weighted graph that connects all vertices with the minimum total weight possible, without forming any cycles. It's like finding the cheapest way to connect all points in a network while ensuring everything stays connected.

content.question: How does the reverse deletion algorithm work to find an MST?

content.text: Union Find is a data structure that efficiently tracks connected components in a dynamic graph. content.text: Quick Find uses an array where each element stores its component ID. FIND operations are $O(1)$ since you just check the array. However, UNION operations are $O(n)$ because you must update all elements in one component to match the other component's ID.

Quick Union represents each component as a tree where each node points to its parent. FIND follows parent pointers to the root, while UNION connects one tree's root to another's. Without optimizations, trees can become very tall, making operations slow.

This makes it inefficient for union-heavy workloads.

Quick Union represents each set as a rooted tree, where each element points to its parent and the root points to itself. FIND follows parent pointers to reach the root, while UNION makes one tree's root point to another's root. The main drawback is that trees can become very deep (up to n-1), making operations slow in the worst case.

content.text: Big O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's running time as input size grows. It focuses on the worst-case scenario and ignores constants and lower-order terms. For example, $O(n^2)$ means the algorithm's time grows at most quadratically with input size.

Omega (Ω) notation provides a lower bound on an algorithm's running time. It describes the best-case scenario - the minimum time an algorithm will take. While Big O gives us the ceiling, $\Omega$ gives us the floor of performance.

Theta (Θ) notation represents tight bounds on running time - both upper and lower bounds simultaneously. When an algorithm is $\Theta(n)$, it means the running time grows exactly proportionally to n, not faster or slower.

Asymptotic analysis compares how algorithms scale as input size approaches infinity. Constants become irrelevant - an $O(n)$ algorithm will always outperform $O(n^2)$ for sufficiently large inputs, regardless of constant factors.

In asymptotic notation, we ignore constant factors because they become insignificant for large inputs. An algorithm taking 1000n steps is still $O(n)$, and will eventually outperform one taking $n^2$ steps as n grows large enough.

content.text: Probabilistic analysis uses probability theory to analyze algorithm performance on random inputs. Instead of worst-case guarantees, we compute expected running time over all possible inputs. This approach is especially useful when worst-case analysis is too pessimistic or when dealing with randomized algorithms.

Randomized algorithms make random choices during execution, using probability to achieve good performance. They contrast with deterministic algorithms by introducing randomness as a tool. Even with the same input, a randomized algorithm may produce different outputs or take different execution paths.

The hiring problem demonstrates probabilistic analysis through a scenario of hiring the best candidate. We interview candidates sequentially and must decide immediately whether to hire each one. This models many online algorithms where decisions must be made without knowing future inputs.

Expected running time averages performance over all possible inputs, weighted by their probability. This often provides more realistic performance estimates than worst-case analysis. For many practical applications, expected case analysis better reflects real-world algorithm behavior.

content.text: A heap is a complete binary tree that satisfies the heap property: in a max-heap, each parent node is greater than or equal to its children. Heaps are typically implemented as arrays for efficiency. They're fundamental for priority queues and heapsort algorithm.

Heapsort first builds a max-heap from the input array, then repeatedly extracts the maximum element and places it at the end. This creates a sorted array in $O(n \log n)$ time with $O(1)$ space complexity. It's an in-place, comparison-based sorting algorithm.

A priority queue is an abstract data type where elements have priorities and are served in priority order. Heaps provide an efficient implementation with $O(\log n)$ insertion and deletion. Maximum priority elements are accessed first in $O(1)$ time.

Building a heap from an unsorted array can be done in $O(n)$ time using bottom-up approach. Start from the last non-leaf node and apply heapify downward to each node. This is more efficient than inserting elements one by one.

Max-heapify maintains the heap property by comparing a node with its children and swapping if necessary. It then recursively applies to the affected subtree. The operation runs in $O(\log n)$ time and is fundamental to heap operations.

content.text: Quicksort is a divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm that picks a pivot element and partitions the array around it. Elements smaller than the pivot go left, larger elements go right. It then recursively sorts the subarrays, achieving efficient $O(n \log n)$ average-case performance.

Quicksort follows the classic divide-and-conquer paradigm by breaking the sorting problem into smaller subproblems. After partitioning around a pivot, it recursively solves two smaller sorting problems. The combination step is trivial since elements are sorted in-place during partitioning.

The partition operation rearranges the array so all elements less than the pivot come before it, and all greater elements come after. This ensures the pivot is in its final sorted position. The partition operation runs in $O(n)$ time and is the core of quicksort's efficiency.

Unlike merge sort, quicksort sorts in-place using only $O(\log n)$ extra space for recursion stack. This makes it memory-efficient for large datasets. The in-place partitioning swaps elements within the original array without requiring additional storage proportional to input size.

Randomized quicksort chooses the pivot randomly instead of using a fixed strategy like first or last element. This eliminates dependence on input order and guarantees $O(n \log n)$ expected time complexity regardless of input distribution, making worst-case scenarios extremely unlikely.

content.text: A stack is a linear data structure that follows the Last In, First Out (LIFO) principle. Elements are added and removed from the same end, called the top. Think of it like a stack of plates - you can only take the top plate off.

A queue is a linear data structure that follows the First In, First Out (FIFO) principle. Elements are added at the rear and removed from the front. It's like a line at a store - the first person in line is the first to be served.
Array-based implementations of stacks and queues offer constant-time $O(1)$ operations and efficient memory usage. However, they have fixed capacity and may require resizing when full.

Linked list implementations provide dynamic size allocation and no memory waste. Each node contains data and a pointer to the next node, allowing efficient insertion and deletion without shifting elements.

Rooted trees can be represented using parent pointers, child lists, or left-child right-sibling representation. Each method has trade-offs between space efficiency and operation complexity depending on the specific use case.

content.text: Direct-address tables use keys as array indices for $O(1)$ operations. They work well when the key universe is small and reasonably dense. Each key k corresponds directly to slot k in the table.
Hash tables use hash functions to map keys to array indices, enabling average $O(1)$ operations. They handle large key universes efficiently by compressing keys into a smaller range of indices.
Collisions occur when different keys hash to the same slot. Two main resolution strategies exist: chaining (storing multiple elements per slot) and open addressing (finding alternative slots).
Load factor α = n/m represents the average number of elements per slot. It directly affects hash table performance - low load factors improve performance but waste space.

Universal hashing randomly selects hash functions from a carefully designed family. This approach guarantees good average performance regardless of input, preventing worst-case scenarios from adversarial inputs.

content.text: Amortized analysis provides a way to analyze the average performance of operations over a sequence, even when individual operations may be expensive. It gives a more realistic view of algorithm performance than worst-case analysis alone. This technique is particularly useful for data structures where occasional expensive operations are balanced by many cheap ones.

Some algorithms have operations that are occasionally expensive but mostly cheap. Traditional worst-case analysis can be overly pessimistic in these cases. Amortized analysis shows that the expensive operations are rare enough that the average cost per operation is low, providing a more accurate performance guarantee.

Dynamic tables automatically resize to accommodate varying numbers of elements, growing when full and shrinking when mostly empty. While individual resize operations are expensive, amortized analysis shows that the average cost per insertion or deletion remains constant. This makes dynamic arrays practical despite occasional expensive resizing.

The potential method assigns a potential function to data structures that represents stored energy for future expensive operations. When cheap operations occur, potential increases; when expensive operations occur, potential decreases to pay for the extra cost. The amortized cost equals actual cost plus the change in potential.

Both accounting and potential methods achieve the same goal of amortized analysis but differ in approach. The accounting method assigns credits to individual operations, while the potential method assigns a global potential function to the entire data structure. The potential method is often more mathematically elegant and easier to verify.

content.text: A minimum spanning tree (MST) is a subset of edges in a connected, weighted graph that connects all vertices with the minimum total edge weight. It forms a tree structure with exactly n-1 edges for n vertices, containing no cycles.
The generic MST algorithm grows the spanning tree by adding safe edges one at a time. A safe edge is one that can be added to the current set of edges without violating the MST property.
The cut property states that for any cut in a graph, the minimum weight edge crossing the cut is safe for the MST. This fundamental theorem underlies both Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms.
Kruskal's algorithm uses Union-Find to detect cycles efficiently. Before adding an edge, it checks if the endpoints are in the same connected component using Find operations.
An MST is unique if and only if all edge weights in the graph are distinct. When edge weights are repeated, multiple MSTs with the same total weight may exist.

content.text: Standard matrix multiplication of two n×n matrices takes $O(n^3)$ time using the naive algorithm. Each element requires n multiplications and additions. Advanced algorithms like Strassen's can reduce this to approximately $O(n^2.807)$.

Gaussian elimination solves systems of linear equations by transforming the coefficient matrix into row echelon form. The algorithm runs in $O(n^3)$ time for an n×n system. It involves forward elimination followed by back substitution.

LU decomposition factors a matrix A into lower triangular L and upper triangular U matrices where A = LU. Once computed in $O(n^3)$ time, solving multiple systems with the same coefficient matrix takes only $O(n^2)$ per solution.

Computing the inverse of an n×n matrix requires $O(n^3)$ time using Gaussian elimination or LU decomposition. However, explicitly computing the inverse is often unnecessary and numerically unstable compared to solving linear systems directly.

For symmetric positive-definite matrices, Cholesky decomposition provides a more efficient factorization than general LU decomposition. It runs in $O(n^3/3)$ time, roughly half the work of LU decomposition, and uses only $O(n^2)$ storage.

content.text: The Euclidean algorithm for computing GCD runs in $O(log min(a,b)$) time. This logarithmic complexity makes it extremely efficient even for very large numbers. The algorithm's performance is based on the fact that the Fibonacci sequence represents the worst-case input.

points: Extended Euclidean algorithm finds coefficients x,y where gcd(a,b) = ax + by

content.text: Computing a^b mod n using repeated squaring takes $O(log b)$ multiplications instead of $O(b)$. This exponential improvement is crucial for cryptographic applications like RSA. Without this optimization, cryptographic operations would be computationally infeasible.

content.question: What is the time complexity of solving a system of congruences using the Chinese Remainder Theorem?
content.answer: $O(k lo$g^2$N)$ where k is the number of congruences and N is the product of all moduli. This includes k applications of the extended Euclidean algorithm.

content.text: Deterministic primality testing has complexity $O(n^(1/2)$) for trial division, but probabilistic methods like Miller-Rabin achieve $O(k lo$g^3$n)$ where k is the number of rounds. Modern polynomial-time deterministic algorithms exist but are less practical than probabilistic ones.

content.question: What is the time complexity of solving the modular linear equation ax ≡ b (mod n)?
content.answer: $O(\log n)$ using the extended Euclidean algorithm to find the modular inverse of a, assuming gcd(a,n) = 1.

content.text: String matching finds all occurrences of a pattern string P within a text string T. The goal is to locate every position where P appears as a substring in T. This fundamental problem appears in text editors, search engines, and bioinformatics applications.

points: Checks pattern against text at every possible position
Time complexity is $O(nm)$ where n is text length and m is pattern length
Simple to implement but inefficient for large inputs
No preprocessing required - starts matching immediately
Worst case occurs when pattern and text have many matching characters

content.text: Rabin-Karp uses rolling hash functions to efficiently compare pattern and text substrings. It computes hash values for the pattern and each text window, comparing hashes first before character-by-character verification. Expected time complexity is $O(n+m)$ but worst case is $O(nm)$.

content.question: What is a rolling hash in the context of string matching?
content.answer: A rolling hash allows efficient computation of hash values for overlapping substrings by incrementally updating the hash as the window slides, removing the leftmost character and adding the new rightmost character.

points: Builds a finite state machine that recognizes the pattern
Preprocessing takes $O($m^3$σ)$ time where σ is alphabet size
Matching phase runs in $O(n)$ time with no backtracking
Each character in text causes exactly one state transition
Guarantees linear time matching once automaton is built

content.question: What is the worst-case time complexity of the naive string matching algorithm?

content.text: KMP avoids redundant comparisons by preprocessing the pattern to create a failure function. When a mismatch occurs, it uses this function to determine how far to shift the pattern without missing potential matches. Achieves $O(n+m)$ time complexity with no backtracking in the text.

content.question: What does the failure function in KMP algorithm represent?
content.answer: The failure function π[i] gives the length of the longest proper prefix of P[1..i] that is also a suffix of P[1..i], enabling optimal pattern shifting when mismatches occur.

Requires $O(n \log n)$ time to construct using efficient algorithms
Enables fast pattern matching queries in $O(m \log n)$ time

content.text: Most efficient string matching algorithms invest time in preprocessing either the pattern or text to enable faster matching. KMP preprocesses the pattern to build a failure function, while suffix arrays preprocess the text. This upfront cost pays off when performing multiple searches.

content.text: K-means clustering partitions data into k clusters by iteratively assigning points to nearest centroids and updating centroid positions. The algorithm runs in $O(nkt)$ time where n is data points, k is clusters, and t is iterations. It's widely used for unsupervised learning and data analysis tasks.

points: K-means has $O(nkt)$ time complexity per iteration

content.text: The multiplicative weights algorithm maintains probability distributions over experts or strategies, updating weights multiplicatively based on performance. Each iteration takes $O(n)$ time to update n weights. This approach provides strong theoretical guarantees for online learning and approximation algorithms.

content.question: What is the time complexity of k-means clustering algorithm?
content.answer: $O(nkt)$ where n is the number of data points, k is the number of clusters, and t is the number of iterations until convergence.

content.text: Gradient descent optimizes functions by iteratively moving in the direction of steepest descent. content.text: The class P contains all decision problems that can be solved by a deterministic algorithm in polynomial time. These are problems where we can find a solution in $O(n^k)$ time for some constant k. Examples include sorting, shortest path, and minimum spanning tree.

NP is the class of decision problems for which a 'yes' answer can be verified in polynomial time given a certificate (proof). While we might not know how to solve these problems quickly, we can check if a proposed solution is correct efficiently.
NP-Complete problems are the hardest problems in NP - every problem in NP can be reduced to them in polynomial time. If any NP-Complete problem can be solved in polynomial time, then P = NP.
A polynomial-time reduction transforms one problem into another in polynomial time. If problem A reduces to problem B and B is solvable in polynomial time, then A is also solvable in polynomial time.

The Cook-Levin theorem proved that Boolean Satisfiability (SAT) is NP-Complete, establishing the first known NP-Complete problem. This landmark result opened the door to proving other problems NP-Complete through reductions from SAT.

points: P: Problems solvable in polynomial time
NP: Problems verifiable in polynomial time
NP-Complete: Hardest problems in NP
NP-Hard: At least as hard as NP-Complete problems

content.question: What does the P vs NP question ask?
content.answer: Whether every problem whose solution can be verified quickly can also be solved quickly. In other words, does P equal NP?

content.question: What is a certificate in the context of NP problems?
content.answer: A certificate is additional information that helps verify a 'yes' answer to a decision problem in polynomial time. It serves as proof that the answer is correct.

content.text: Approximation algorithms provide near-optimal solutions to NP-hard optimization problems when exact solutions are computationally infeasible. They guarantee solutions within a certain factor of the optimal solution, making them practical for real-world applications where perfect accuracy can be traded for computational efficiency.

points: Find minimum set of vertices that covers all edges in a graph
NP-hard optimization problem with practical applications in network monitoring
2-approximation algorithm exists using greedy edge selection
Optimal solution requires exponential time for large graphs
Used in network security and resource allocation problems

content.text: The TSP seeks the shortest route visiting all cities exactly once and returning to the start. This classic NP-hard problem has no polynomial-time exact algorithm, but approximation algorithms can find solutions within 1.5 times optimal for metric instances using minimum spanning trees.

content.question: What is an approximation ratio in the context of approximation algorithms?
content.answer: The approximation ratio is the maximum factor by which an approximation algorithm's solution can exceed the optimal solution. For example, a 2-approximation algorithm guarantees a solution at most twice the optimal cost.

points: Find minimum number of subsets that cover all elements in universe
Greedy algorithm achieves ln(n)-approximation ratio
Applications include facility location and resource allocation
No polynomial-time algorithm exists unless P = NP

content.text: Randomized approximation algorithms use probability to achieve better expected performance than deterministic approaches. They can provide solutions with high probability guarantees and often achieve better approximation ratios, especially useful when dealing with online problems or large datasets.

content.question: How is linear programming used in approximation algorithms?
content.answer: Linear programming relaxation converts integer programming problems into continuous variable problems that can be solved optimally in polynomial time. The LP solution provides bounds and can be rounded to obtain approximate integer solutions.

points: Determine if subset of integers sums to target value
NP-complete decision problem with optimization variant
Fully polynomial-time approximation scheme (FPTAS) exists

content.question: What is the best known approximation ratio for the metric traveling salesperson problem?

content.text: A Fully Polynomial-Time Approximation Scheme (FPTAS) is an approximation algorithm that, for any ε > 0, produces a (1+ε)-approximation in time polynomial in both the input size and 1/ε. This represents the best possible approximation guarantee for NP-hard problems.

content.question: Why are greedy algorithms commonly used for approximation problems?
content.answer: Greedy algorithms are efficient, easy to implement, and often provide good approximation ratios. They make locally optimal choices at each step, which frequently leads to globally reasonable solutions for optimization problems, even when optimal solutions are computationally intractable.
